sentences.text: Yes.
Yes, one bulb.
No, it was a big room.
One, one bulb in the room.
No, nothing.
We had a room and a half.
The main room was everything, the kitchen there, the, the [INAUDIBLE], the, the closet, the this, the that, the box, the box where they keep the coal and the wood in it.
You know, so this was the big room.
And then it was just beds to sleep.
In the next.
Well, it was a little like this maybe, this size.
Oh, we were sleeping together in the beds.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
They slept in-- over there, too.
No.
It was in the hallway, only one faucet--
--for everybody.
It was 10, 12 tenants, whatever.
A telephone?
You're kidding.
[LAUGHS] No telephone.
I, I, I-- no, I didn't-- I never heard anything, no.
Nothing.
Nothing.
I don't know nothing about it.
I didn't know nothing about it.
I never heard.
I don't know.
Maybe they talk between them, and the kids not to hear.
But it was-- I had a big brother, and under him, you know, I had a brother.
He was already back from the army, finished now in Polish Army.
Maybe between them.
They were talking between them.
But, you know, a kid, ah, what you have in your mind.
Oh, no.
Hershl.
Yeah.
What, what they-- yeah.
The only thing what they discussed is, is that we should leave here, the places, and run away.
And they were talking, and-- but they didn't have the ability to do it.
So some people left, I remember.
They, they hid-- they hold it a secret.
You know, kids may be talking too much or whatever.
So people that went to Israel already, I remember this.
When they went to Israel, it was secret between the older people.
They were going.
They were going.
Yeah, but they were going illegal.
Yeah.
No, I start being over there, a member.
My brother was a member there, not the oldest-- a little one behind him.
Never did tell me anything.
He knew more.
He was already-- I don't know.
He was 18, 17?
I don't know.
I never heard-- maybe yes.
I don't remember.
I don't 'member.
How many times I didn't remember my name.
I'm not kidding.
Some Germans, they [NON-ENGLISH].
What's your name?
You know, [NON-ENGLISH].
Well, as soon they came in.
Yeah.
I remember that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember when the war broke out, a lot of Jews start walkin' towards Russia.
From Lodz.
But you didn't see such a masses.
Singles there you hear, here and there.
You know, oh my father [NON-ENGLISH].
My [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, so that's all what you heard.
But when the Germans came in with-- this I remember.
It stays in front of me-- with the motorcycles.
And the guy sits on the other side here, and with the rifles here, the machine guns, this, and this, and this.
And all the goyim, clapping and flowers thrown on them.
And we stood down there.
And not even a day, they start beating us up, breaking all the stores, breaking everything.
You were afraid to go out even, afraid to go out.
And the little stinkers like this, they wore a swastik already.
Next day, swastik, Polacks, swastik.
And they used to say, boy, oh boy.
We going to pay you now.
And they broke the glasses in the stores and the doors, and you are afraid to go out.
You beaten-- they beat you up, little stinkers.
The boy with sticks can hit with the [NON-ENGLISH].
I was afraid with the [NON-ENGLISH].
It was murder already.
And you knew this is the problem.
Nothing, we couldn't do nothing.
No.
We were in the house.
I was going out, looking around this, and run back.
Because you, you didn't have a way to buy a piece of bread already.
They burned everything.
They loot everything.
They, they destroyed everything.
There was-- the, the bakers they were blocking everything with iron, with this and that to bake a bread or something.
And this would happen.
And we saw trouble.
Thousands of people were walking towards Russia.
No.
So my brother, my brother, they, they took him to the army when the war broke out.
And the one behind him-- I don't know, two years different or whatever-- he went with him.
And my mother said it's OK.
[NON-ENGLISH], you know, go save yourself.
So they went close to-- not far already from Russia.
And he changed his mind, the younger one.
He said, no.
I'm not going.
What am I going to do there?
Where?
So he said, do what you want.
My brother, he knew the Germans.
He was in the army there in the war.
And he smuggled out.
They, they caught him.
He, he had blond hair and blue eyes, and he looked like a goy.
And he speak better Polish than the Polack, intelligent Polish.
You have two types of Polish, you know, like country or, or real clear and nice.
So he said, no.
I know them.
This is [NON-ENGLISH].
They're going to kill every Jew.
He goes.
So he went across.
He went to Russia?
He went to Russia and survived in Russia.
He came back.
And I think in '41 this happened that we start wearing a band or a [NON-ENGLISH] here.
They, they vote out for, for the president-- not the Jewish made.
This, you know, it's a funny thing.
You know, you had a lot of rich people in the ghetto, Jewish people.
One rich woman was my--
--aunt.
Rich, rich, rich.
I was in the ghetto.
The street was in the ghetto.
Yeah.
I was in the ghetto.
They poked-- they picked a, a poor neighborhood.
And this was the place there.
No, no, no.
Everybody lived where they were.
But there was plenty empty.
Because the Polacks, they went out from the ghetto.
So they had plenty of space for the Jews.
Nobody could go to work.
Didn't have a school, didn't have anything.
And for a while, you know, little by little you didn't have what to eat.
Oh, oh, oh, a city.
Huge, huge.
Really big.
Me?
Nobody had.
Yes.
He was going in a nice, beautiful horse and a buggy, you know, a closed one, nice.
A carriage, and this was he's driving in it.
But this idiot, he had the Magen David here, too.
And he was a son of a bitch, excuse me.
A bitch.
And the--
--and the Sonderkommando, you know, the police, this was our whole underworld.
Jewish people in the ghetto.
The underworld, therefore crooks.
In what way?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The, the people from the, from-- they killed the, the cows, the animals to eat.
They were brutal.
People, they had no other money, no heart, you know.
Those people, and this was a clique.
And those people used to be the policemen.
And they worked together.
They thought maybe they're going to be better than-- treated by the Germans than those poor people or average person.
So they were tough.
They were tough.
That didn't work.
Well, I don't know if you are interesting in this right now.
But, you know, this brings me back to see what people can do, like I was-- my brother died, and I came.
Your brother died in--
When he, when he died, but this was already--
--later in the ghetto.
This is later.
Everybody, whatever who died is in the ghetto, outside not.
Oh, we're young.
So what happen is when this brother, my brother died, and I went to work for him, I, I saw--
Nothing, was no work.
No.
No, this was-- this was a part in the ghetto that the, the, the train, the cargo that used to bring in the food, the little food what came, or coal or wood, came in over there.
They unload it, and they rationed this.
Each time everybody got this and this and this and this--
A pound of potatoes or, or a little coal or a little-- some bread, whatever they delivered, you know.
So he used to-- not just he-- 100 people, 200 people, it was big.
You know?
And he used to unload.
Two people took a whole cargo, one, one, one-- what do you call this--
A car.
So he used to be good, and they liked him.
So he used to work there.
I don't know.
He got the job.
No, no.
Probably it was the leaders from there.
There was a head of it.
I remember what his name was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We killed him in Rome after the war.
A Jew.
We killed this guy in Rome, Lederman-Lederman.
He was the head of it over there.
Unloading.
Yeah.
Germans.
But I'm sure he, he knew everything, because he was a big, big man.
No, no, no.
Just Jewish.
Garbage, garbage, what came there.
Garbage.
Yeah.
It was apart from the ghetto, but was a big place where the train goes in.
They, they, they moved everything, download it, till they, they spread this in the stores where they have to supply for the people, the Jewish.
So it was a nice size of this.
Yeah.
He came home one day, and he said he has trouble with the knee.
And he couldn't walk.
So my sister says-- my mother was already very sad-- very, very, very like done.
She saw, like, an end to it, you know, the whole thing in '43.
You know, so she saw an end to it.
And so she was not that, you know, effective, you know.
So my sister says, you know, Melech, come on, let's go to the hospital, some hospital.
So they took him to the hospital.
And she left him there, and that's it.
They just died there.
People [INAUDIBLE].
You never visited him in the hospital?
No.
We didn't even know where this is and was nothing.
You're [INAUDIBLE] in like an animal.
And I went there, and I said I'm Melech's brother, a Jewish guy, a Jewish guy.
Not this head.
No, it was another two, two people that were lower that-- closer to the people, go do this, do that.
The big shot was-- in the beginning I thought he's not Jewish even, this big guy with the boots, you know, high.
I remember here, nice, nice, Polish military boots with the pants like this, you know, white here.
So we finished this guy in, in Rome.
How long did you work in the unloading and loading there?
I worked quite a long time.
I don't know-- eight, nine, 10 months.
I was-- if you want to hear a nice little story, too-- you might have forgot all about it.
Now you say, in this place where I unload-- so I knew my brother, they used to give him every month about four or five days to go in a bakery, to work in a bakery.
Over there, you get a piece of bread, enough to eat.
So it was no work.
So he went there, and he had bread enough to eat.
And he used to steal home the bread, some bread, hide.
In the bakeries, they used to count the bread.
The Sonderkommando, those police, they were sitting two guys that had the keys to the flour.
And they count the bread, how much bread goes into the oven, how much bread comes out from the oven that nobody steals and gets the bread.
So now I remember, you know, it was a long hallway, long, the whole length of the bakery.
One room was-- I don't know-- offices.
And then when there was a table on the side, the two police were sitting always on this side.
Here was a little room that the workers were sitting and eating.
And then another, and the whole length, and then the end over there it was a tank with water to making the flour, the whole thing.
And the next room is where they're putting in, in this pot the machine that--
--turns the flour and makes the dough and, and mixes over there.
So they used to send me.
So I got the first time they sent me, I was good.
Send me for a week, five days, go in the bakery, vacation.
So I worked in the bakery.
I know that my-- everybody is laying there dead, no food.
And here is so much bread.
My father was a, a finger, toothpick, a toothpick.
He couldn't even move the, already the, the jaw.
Dead people.
And the brother that came back, he didn't want to go.
So he was laying in this side of the bed.
He was laying in this side, dead people, dead.
Nothing to do, nothing to eat.
And my mother, nothing.
Nothing, kids, nobody had to eat, nothing.
Nothing to eat, really nothing-- a week, two weeks.
Nothing.
You eat grass.
You pick grass.
You did.
You-- I don't know.
So I, I, I was crazy in this bakery.
So what I did, so I used to go and when the dough-- they made the dough, the machines-- nobody were there.
Just [INAUDIBLE] you know?
Yeah.
So I used to go and rip a piece from this one.
To the next one, where the fire is burning from the wood to cook this.
And I used to put the coal on the side.
And I used to put this in.
If they ever seen me, they would give me a bullet in the head outside.
But I took a chance.
I didn't think.
Rumkowski people, yeah.
No.
I saw him in the streets a few times going back and forth.
I saw him, I saw him coming to Auschwitz.
And, and, and then behind him, and the Germans they were nice.
Who is your relatives?
Oh, everybody, everybody had a whole bunch sticking to him.
When he came to Auschwitz.
No, no, no, oh, he came back later, later, later, when I was there already, when they closed the ghetto, when they closed the ghetto already.
I don't know what-- which transport this was, after me.
But they finished the ghetto and--
Yeah.
Yeah.
Mr.-- did-- could you bribe Rumkowski if--
I don't know.
I was--
Not just me, even other people.
This Rumkowski had just met the, the, the, the [NON-ENGLISH], they used to call them, the top of it, you know, the top.
That's all.
Some people, maybe.
I don't know.
Yeah, yeah.
You don't know about that.
Were, were there-- were you in a [NON-ENGLISH] or an [NON-ENGLISH] in the ghetto when they took people out?
No, no.
They, they they came, the [INAUDIBLE].
They, they, they closed off the building.
I don't remember if another building.
But they closed everything.
And everybody raus, raus, raus, raus.
And then they went in to check.
And they said take a pot.
Take a fork and a spoon.
You're going to work.
And that's all what they said.
We all went.
I don't know.
My mother put the keys here--
--from the house.
What she hided, I don't know.
He died in the ghetto.
And a brother.
And two brothers.
The rest of it.
Yeah.
There was no funeral.
A lot of people what they did to get their portion bread every week, and many times you didn't get it either.
Because I came to the store.
You stood from 2 o'clock at night by the window outside, cold or warm, whatever.
And then they say, ha, no more.
You always hope that you make it.
You know?
Yes.
So, so what-- I have so many-- so many things in my head comes back.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] If somebody died in the ghetto, there was nobody to take you, nowhere to go.
My father or my brother or the other, what happened with him is they come with a wagon, and people pushed it.
This was a [NON-ENGLISH], a [NON-ENGLISH].
People used to leave it outside on the sidewalk.
People used to hide this.
If you want-- if you lived in the, the beginning--
In the buildings, and they had a hall for cold winter, to have some food fresh, they had halls.
What you call this?
[NON-ENGLISH], cellars, right.
Thank you.
And, and they used to hide them to get the ration, the bread.
You have a card.
And each time they ripped.
So I had it.
So I get more bread.
So if they find out, the police, they beat you to death.
I don't know where.
I don't know where.
They filled up with 10 people, 20 people, whatever they can push.
No.
The ghetto was built.
They closed it.
And I don't remember exactly.
They closed the ghetto in 1940.
It took them almost-- since they, they taking over-- they took over the ghetto, they, they built the ghetto, put concrete poles and everything--
Around it, and when they finished it, they finished at the end of '44, '40, '40.
And they put electric in, high power.
You touched it, you're gone.
Outside they had tall--
Yeah, towers around it.
They supposed to keep the, the, the, the order in the ghetto.
They dealt with dead people.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
Maybe what they knew, maybe relatives.
I don't know.
But you saw them as an animal, as a wild person.
I didn't think that this is even a Jew.
They act even like not Jewish.
Speak Polish.
What do you speak in Yiddish-- in Polish for?
They're so good to you?
But they speak Polish.
What you heard is songs, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people sitting with the feet on the side-- on the sidewalk and singing songs.
You never heard beautiful songs, making songs.
That's all.
No.
There was a, was a song one time in the camp or two, this we used to sing with a, a woman.
You work a whole day, and you come and you-- they-- you wait.
They give you outside a little soup, you know, for the day.
And, and she takes us in the hand and looks and she has nothing to eat.
It's a little water.
So she sings a song.
[NON-ENGLISH], go a little deeper, and get [NON-ENGLISH].
You know?
Thicker stuff a little bit inside.
[NON-ENGLISH SINGING] You know, [LAUGHS] this was the song and another few words there.
But this is what was.
You know?
You turned in plain as an animal.
You see the dog goes, he smells on the floor.
Oh, you find something.
And these were the people.
